content:
# Deuteronomy 27 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA** | EMET vs. LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 26 verses

---

## V.1 — "Commanded, together with the elders"

- **EMET:** "And Moshe commanded, together with the elders of Yisra'el, the people, saying"
- **LOGOS:** "Now Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people, saying"
- **PNEUMA:** "Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people"

**Differences:** EMET emphasizes Moses as subject with elders as accompaniment (va-yetsav Moshe... ve-ziqney Yisra'el). LOGOS makes "Moses and elders" co-subjects. Minor syntactic difference. **Resolution:** EMET's reading is more faithful to the Hebrew syntax; consensus keeps "Moses commanded, together with the elders."

---

## V.2 — "Plaster them with plaster"

- **EMET:** "plaster them with lime"
- **LOGOS:** "plaster them with plaster"
- **PNEUMA:** "coat them with plaster"

**Differences:** EMET: "lime" for sid; LOGOS: "plaster" (preserving the redundancy of the Hebrew vesadta otam be-sid — "you shall plaster them with plaster"); PNEUMA: "coat." **Resolution:** "plaster them with plaster" — LOGOS; the Hebrew reinforcement is worth preserving.

---

## V.3 — "Torah" vs. "instruction" vs. "law"

- **EMET:** "all the words of this Torah"
- **LOGOS:** "all the words of this instruction"
- **PNEUMA:** "all the words of this instruction"

**Differences:** EMET preserves "Torah" (untranslated); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "instruction." Project convention renders torah as "instruction" in most contexts. **Resolution:** "all the words of this instruction" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Note: the project consistently uses "instruction" for torah in Deuteronomy.

---

## V.5 — Iron tool

- **EMET:** "you shall not lift upon them any iron [tool]"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall wield no iron tool on them."
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not use any iron tool on them."

**Differences:** EMET: "lift upon them any iron" — literal rendering of lo tanif aleihem barzel. LOGOS: "wield no iron tool." PNEUMA: "use any iron tool." The Hebrew barzel means simply "iron" — no word for "tool" appears. **Resolution:** "you shall not wield any iron upon them" — preserves the literalness without adding "tool."

---

## V.6 — "Uncut stones" vs. "whole stones"

- **EMET:** "Of whole stones you shall build the altar"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall build the altar of the LORD your God of uncut stones"
- **PNEUMA:** "Build the altar of the LORD your God with fieldstones"

**Differences:** EMET: "whole stones" (avanot shlemot — from shalem, complete/whole); LOGOS: "uncut stones"; PNEUMA: "fieldstones." The Hebrew shlemot means "complete/whole" (not worked by tools). LOGOS's "uncut" captures the functional meaning; EMET's "whole/uncut stones" is closer. **Resolution:** "Of uncut stones you shall build the altar" — LOGOS; "uncut" captures the prohibition of iron tools.

---

## V.7 — "Peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offerings"

- **EMET/LOGOS:** "peace offerings" (shelamim)
- **PNEUMA:** "fellowship offerings"

**Differences:** PNEUMA renders shelamim as "fellowship offerings" (a common modern translation convention, notably in NIV). EMET/LOGOS: "peace offerings." **Resolution:** "peace offerings" — traditional rendering of shelamim, consistent with project conventions.

---

## V.9 — Opening call to attention

- **EMET:** "Be silent and listen, O Yisra'el"
- **LOGOS:** "Keep silence and hear, O Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "Be silent, Israel, and listen!"

**Differences:** EMET: "Be silent and listen"; LOGOS: "Keep silence and hear"; PNEUMA adds exclamation mark. The Hebrew hasket ushma conveys a solemn hush. **Resolution:** "Be silent and listen, O Israel" — EMET.

---

## V.9 — "Have become" vs. "are"

- **EMET:** "this day you have become a people for the LORD your God"
- **LOGOS:** "this day you have become the people of the LORD your God"
- **PNEUMA:** "You have now become the people of the LORD your God"

**Differences:** EMET: "a people for" (le-am); LOGOS: "the people of" — adding the definite article not in the Hebrew. PNEUMA: "now" for ha-yom ("today/this day"). **Resolution:** "this day you have become a people for the LORD your God" — EMET; the indefinite "a people" may be more accurate.

---

## V.10 — "Obey" vs. "listen to"

- **EMET:** "And you shall listen to the voice of the LORD your God and do his commandments"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall therefore obey the voice of the LORD your God, keeping his commandments"
- **PNEUMA:** "Obey the LORD your God and follow his commands and decrees"

**Differences:** EMET: "listen to the voice" (shamata be-qol) — more literal; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "obey." PNEUMA collapses "voice of the LORD" to "the LORD." **Resolution:** "And you shall listen to the voice of the LORD your God and do his commandments" — EMET; preserves the voice/obedience connection.

---

## V.14 — "Declare" vs. "answer and say"

- **EMET:** "the Levites shall answer and say to every man of Yisra'el with a loud voice"
- **LOGOS:** "the Levites shall declare to all the men of Israel in a loud voice"
- **PNEUMA:** "The Levites shall recite to all the people of Israel in a loud voice"

**Differences:** EMET: "answer and say" (ve-anu ve-amru); LOGOS: "declare"; PNEUMA: "recite." The Hebrew anu ve-amru means "shall answer and say" — implying a responsive liturgy. **Resolution:** "the Levites shall answer and say to all the men of Israel in a loud voice" — EMET.

---

## V.15 — "Cursed be the man" formulation

- **EMET:** "Cursed [arur] is the man who makes..."
- **LOGOS:** "Cursed be the man who makes..."
- **PNEUMA:** "Cursed is anyone who makes..."

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS: "the man" (ha-ish); PNEUMA: "anyone." The Hebrew specifies ish. LOGOS: "Cursed be" (declaratory subjunctive) vs. EMET: "Cursed is." **Resolution:** "Cursed be the man who makes" (v.15) — LOGOS; declaratory passive is the proper form of arur. For subsequent curses (vv.16–26) "Cursed be the one who" where the Hebrew uses participial construction.

---

## V.15 — "Sets it in secret" — the location

- **EMET:** "and sets [it] in secret"
- **LOGOS:** "and sets it up in secret"
- **PNEUMA:** "and sets it up in secret" (same as LOGOS)

**Differences:** Minor: EMET brackets [it] indicating "sets in secret" without explicit object. LOGOS/PNEUMA add "up." **Resolution:** "and sets it up in secret" — LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## V.17 — "Boundary stone" vs. "landmark"

- **EMET:** "removes his neighbor's boundary marker"
- **LOGOS:** "moves his neighbor's landmark"
- **PNEUMA:** "moves their neighbor's boundary stone"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "removes" (masig); LOGOS: "moves"; PNEUMA: "moves." The Hebrew hisgig gebul re'ehu — "moves the boundary of his neighbor." "Moves" is accurate; "removes" implies complete removal. (2) EMET: "boundary marker"; LOGOS: "landmark"; PNEUMA: "boundary stone." The physical referent is a stone marker (gebul). **Resolution:** "moves his neighbor's boundary stone" — PNEUMA; "moves" and "boundary stone" are both accurate.

---

## V.18 — "Misleads" vs. "causes to wander" vs. "leads astray"

- **EMET:** "causes the blind to wander from the road"
- **LOGOS:** "misleads a blind person on the road"
- **PNEUMA:** "leads the blind astray on the road"

**Differences:** EMET: "causes... to wander" (mashgeh) — more literal of the Hiphil of shagah (to cause to stray). LOGOS: "misleads"; PNEUMA: "leads astray." **Resolution:** "misleads the blind on the road" — LOGOS.

---

## V.19 — "Perverts justice" vs. "withholds justice"

- **EMET/LOGOS:** "perverts the justice of the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow"
- **PNEUMA:** "withholds justice from the foreigner, the fatherless, or the widow"

**Differences:** (1) EMET/LOGOS: "perverts" (matteh mishpat) — the Hebrew verb is natah, to bend/turn aside. PNEUMA: "withholds" — different nuance. (2) PNEUMA: "foreigner" for ger; project convention is "sojourner." **Resolution:** "perverts the justice of the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

## V.20 — "Uncovered the covering" vs. "dishonored his father's bed"

- **EMET:** "because he has uncovered the covering of his father"
- **LOGOS:** "because he has uncovered his father's covering"
- **PNEUMA:** "for he dishonors his father's bed"

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS: "uncovered the covering of his father" (galah kenaf aviv) — the Hebrew idiom "uncover the skirt/wing of his father" = exposed his father's sexual domain. PNEUMA: "dishonors his father's bed" — interpretive, not literal. **Resolution:** "because he has uncovered his father's covering" — LOGOS.

---

## V.21 — "Any beast" vs. "any animal" vs. "any kind of animal"

- **EMET:** "lies with any beast"
- **LOGOS:** "lies with any kind of animal"
- **PNEUMA:** "has sexual relations with any animal"

**Differences:** EMET: "beast" for behemah; LOGOS: "any kind of animal" (adds "kind of"); PNEUMA: "has sexual relations with" — more explicit. **Resolution:** "lies with any animal" — removes archaic "beast" while retaining the simpler phrasing.

---

## V.24 — "Strikes" vs. "strikes down" vs. "kills"

- **EMET:** "strikes his neighbor in secret"
- **LOGOS:** "strikes down his neighbor in secret"
- **PNEUMA:** "kills their neighbor in secret"

**Differences:** EMET: "strikes" (makkeh) — the Hebrew Hiphil of nkh means to strike/smite (not necessarily kill). LOGOS: "strikes down"; PNEUMA: "kills" — goes too far. The curse does not require death. **Resolution:** "strikes his neighbor in secret" — EMET.

---

## V.25 — "Innocent blood" vs. "innocent person"

- **EMET:** "to strike down an innocent person [nephesh]"
- **LOGOS:** "to shed innocent blood"
- **PNEUMA:** "to kill an innocent person" / "accepts a bribe to kill an innocent person"

**Differences:** EMET: "strike down an innocent person" with [nephesh] gloss. LOGOS: "shed innocent blood" — the standard idiomatic rendering of shefokh dam naqiy. PNEUMA: "kill." The Hebrew le-hakkot nephesh dam naqiy — "to smite the life of innocent blood." **Resolution:** "takes a bribe to shed innocent blood" — LOGOS; established idiomatic phrase.

---

## V.26 — "Uphold" vs. "confirm" vs. "does not keep"

- **EMET:** "does not uphold the words of this Torah, to do them"
- **LOGOS:** "does not confirm the words of this instruction by doing them"
- **PNEUMA:** "does not uphold the words of this instruction by carrying them out"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "Torah" (transliterated); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "instruction." (2) LOGOS: "confirm" (yaqim — Hiphil of qum, "cause to stand/establish"); EMET/PNEUMA: "uphold." LOGOS's "confirm" is more precise etymologically; "uphold" is more idiomatic. **Resolution:** "does not uphold the words of this instruction by doing them" — PNEUMA; "uphold" is idiomatic and "instruction" is project standard.

---

*Total disagreements logged: 19*
*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
